title: SQL Full Course - Build a Database and Deploy It
summary: An AWS tutorial video covering foundational SQL concepts, database design, and hands-on deployment to the cloud. Covers CRUD operations, normalization, relationships, JOINs, and deploying MySQL databases to Amazon RDS.
writeup: A ground-up introduction to relational databases using MySQL, tracing every concept from the four CRUD operations through hands-on SQL table design and data insertion. The video installs MySQL and MySQL Workbench on both Mac (Homebrew) and Windows, creates a database and a users table with typed columns, constraints, and an auto-incrementing primary key, then walks through INSERT and SELECT queries against a live schema. The through-line is that the same 1970s relational model underlies every tap, swipe, and form submission in modern apps.
key_points: CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete) are the four operations every app performs against a database: account signup is a create, loading a feed is a read, editing a bio is an update, and deleting a post is a delete.
A relational database table mirrors a spreadsheet: each row is one record, each column is a typed field, and every row gets a unique integer primary key (auto-incremented) that no two rows can share.

MySQL Workbench is the GUI layer; MySQL Community Server is the actual database engine. On Mac, Homebrew installs the server with a single command (brew install mysql) and a second command starts it (brew services start mysql); Windows users use the MySQL Community Server installer from dev.mysql.com.

The CREATE TABLE statement defines column names, data types (INT, VARCHAR, TIMESTAMP), and constraints (NOT NULL, UNIQUE, PRIMARY KEY, AUTO_INCREMENT, DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP) all at once, enforcing data integrity before any row is inserted.

INSERT INTO specifies only the columns the application controls (username, email, password); the database fills in the auto-incremented ID and the created_at timestamp automatically, mirroring how an e-commerce system assigns an order number without user input.
SELECT with an asterisk (SELECT * FROM users) returns every column of every row in a table, the R in CRUD, and is the foundation for reading any data the app needs to display.
